date.note: Commonly dated c. 48-50 CE; the year is inferred from the chronology of Paul's letters and Acts and is approximate. Not directly dated in the sources.

summary: The Council of Jerusalem, conventionally dated c. 48-50 CE, was a meeting of leaders of the early Jesus movement over whether non-Jewish (Gentile) converts had to be circumcised and keep the Mosaic law. The two surviving accounts, Acts 15 and Paul's report in Galatians 2, agree that Gentile converts were not required to be circumcised but differ in emphasis and detail.
description: As the Jesus movement spread beyond Jewish communities, a dispute arose over the terms on which non-Jews could belong: whether they had to be circumcised and to keep the Mosaic law, or whether faith in Christ was sufficient. Two early accounts describe a meeting at Jerusalem that addressed this. The Acts of the Apostles (chapter 15) narrates a formal gathering of apostles and elders at which, after debate, Peter and James speak and the assembly issues a letter freeing Gentile believers from circumcision while asking them to abstain from a short list of items (commonly given as things sacrificed to idols, blood, what is strangled, and sexual immorality), the so-called apostolic decree. Paul, in his letter to the Galatians (chapter 2), gives a first-person account of a meeting at Jerusalem with James, Cephas (Peter), and John, at which, he says, his law-free gospel to the Gentiles was recognized and Titus, a Greek, was not compelled to be circumcised; Paul mentions no apostolic decree and stresses instead the agreement over the division of missionary labor and a subsequent conflict with Cephas at Antioch. Historical-critical scholars treat Acts 15 and Galatians 2 as two distinct sources, written by different authors with different aims, and reconstruct the underlying event in various ways: some identify the two accounts as reports of the same meeting, others distinguish the meetings or question the historicity of the apostolic decree in the form Acts gives it. This entry presents both accounts as attributed sources and does not harmonize them into a single narrative asserted as fact. The meeting is widely regarded in scholarship as a decisive moment in the emergence of a Gentile church not bound by circumcision and the ritual law, and thus in the longer process by which the Jesus movement and Judaism came to be distinguished.
editorial_notes: The core neutrality choice here concerns the two sources: Acts 15 and Galatians 2 are presented as distinct, separately attributed accounts that agree on the outcome (Gentiles not required to be circumcised) but differ in emphasis and detail (Acts has a formal decree with dietary and other stipulations; Paul's first-person account has no decree and foregrounds recognition of his mission and the later clash at Antioch). Per neutrality principles #1-2 and the task instruction, the editor does not harmonize them into one narrative asserted as fact, and reports that scholars reconstruct the relationship variously. The date c. 48-50 CE is approximate and inferred from Pauline and Acts chronology, noted as such. Sources triangulate three lenses per #8-9: academic scholarship (Dunn's commentary and monograph on the Antioch incident and the Jerusalem meeting), the primary corpus (Acts 15 and Galatians 2 themselves), and a contemporary scholar reading Paul within Judaism (Fredriksen). No encyclopedias or tertiary sources are cited. Confidence: medium; sources verified to exist and be on-topic, relevant passages of the primary texts consulted for the source difference but the monographs not read line-by-line. Relation targets that exist: paul-of-tarsus (same batch), second-temple-judaism, and parting-of-the-ways. Forward references to not-yet-created entities: peter (Cephas) and james-brother-of-jesus.

relations.note: Paul gives a first-person account of the meeting in Galatians 2 and is a central figure in the Acts 15 narrative; the two accounts differ in emphasis.
Peter (called Cephas by Paul) speaks in the Acts 15 account and is named among the Jerusalem leaders in Galatians 2. Forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.
James, leader of the Jerusalem community, gives the concluding judgment in Acts 15 and is named first among the 'pillars' in Galatians 2. Forward reference to a not-yet-created entity.
The dispute concerned whether Gentile converts must keep the Mosaic law of Second Temple Judaism, including circumcision.
The decision not to require circumcision of Gentile converts is widely treated as a step in the longer process by which the Jesus movement and Judaism came to be distinguished.

sources.citation: The Acts of the Apostles, chapter 15, and Paul, Letter to the Galatians, chapter 2. Primary sources: the two distinct early accounts of the Jerusalem meeting, presented here as separately attributed rather than harmonized. Cited from The Greek New Testament, ed. Aland et al., 5th rev. ed. Stuttgart: Deutsche Bibelgesellschaft, 2014.
sources.license: Underlying texts public domain; critical edition proprietary. Acts 15 and Galatians 2 consulted for the source comparison.

sources.citation: Dunn, James D. G. The Theology of Paul the Apostle. Grand Rapids, MI: William B. Eerdmans, 1998, esp. the treatment of the Jerusalem meeting and the Antioch incident (Galatians 2). Academic exposition weighing the Acts and Galatians accounts of the Gentile question; the outside-scholarship lens.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Fredriksen, Paula. Paul: The Pagans' Apostle. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press, 2017, on the Gentile question and the Jerusalem agreement. Reads the meeting within first-century Judaism and the mission to the nations; the contemporary-scholar lens.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference, relevant passages not read line-by-line.
